items.prompt_md: In the climbing-stairs recurrence, what does dp[i] represent?
options: The number of distinct ways to reach step i
The minimum number of moves needed to reach step i
Whether step i is reachable at all
The number of steps remaining after reaching step i
items.answer: The number of distinct ways to reach step i
items.explanation_md: dp[i] counts every distinct sequence of 1- and 2-steps that lands exactly on step i.

items.prompt_md: Why does dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] correctly count the ways to reach step i?
options: Every way to reach step i arrives either via a final single step from i-1 or a final double step from i-2, and those two cases never overlap
Because addition is the only operation that produces integer counts
Because step i is always adjacent to exactly two other steps in a graph
Because dp[i-1] and dp[i-2] are always equal for large i
items.answer: Every way to reach step i arrives either via a final single step from i-1 or a final double step from i-2, and those two cases never overlap
items.explanation_md: Splitting on the last move taken is what makes the two cases mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive.

items.prompt_md: What base cases does the climbing-stairs table need before the recurrence dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] can run?
options: dp[1] = 1 and dp[2] = 2, since there's exactly one way to reach step 1 and two ways to reach step 2
dp[0] = 1 and dp[1] = 1, matching the standard Fibonacci sequence exactly
Only dp[0] = 0 is needed; the rest follows automatically
No base cases are needed because the recurrence is self-starting
items.answer: dp[1] = 1 and dp[2] = 2, since there's exactly one way to reach step 1 and two ways to reach step 2
items.explanation_md: Without both seed values the loop from i=3 onward has nothing correct to add together.

items.prompt_md: A student writes the naive recursive climb_stairs(n) that calls climb_stairs(n-1) + climb_stairs(n-2) with no memo. Why does this blow up?
options: The same smaller sub-calls (like climb_stairs(n-3)) get recomputed from scratch inside multiple branches of the call tree
Python recursion is always slower than loops regardless of the algorithm
The function runs out of stack space at n=3
Integer overflow forces the interpreter to retry the computation
items.answer: The same smaller sub-calls (like climb_stairs(n-3)) get recomputed from scratch inside multiple branches of the call tree
items.explanation_md: Overlapping subproblems recomputed without caching is exactly what causes the exponential call tree.

items.prompt_md: What turns the naive O(2^n) recursive climbing-stairs solution into an O(n) one, without changing what's being computed?
options: Caching each dp[i] the first time it's computed (via memoization or a bottom-up table) so it's never recomputed
Switching from Python to a faster programming language
Rewriting the recursion to use tail calls instead of a return statement
Sorting the inputs before running the recursion
items.answer: Caching each dp[i] the first time it's computed (via memoization or a bottom-up table) so it's never recomputed
items.explanation_md: Memoization or tabulation both guarantee each of the n subproblems is solved exactly once.

items.prompt_md: What is the key difference between top-down memoization and bottom-up tabulation for this recurrence?
options: Memoization fills the table on demand via recursive calls, while tabulation fills it in a fixed order from the smallest index upward — both visit each subproblem once
Memoization is always faster because it skips unreachable subproblems entirely
Tabulation cannot be used unless the recurrence has exactly one base case
Memoization computes exact answers while tabulation only approximates them
items.answer: Memoization fills the table on demand via recursive calls, while tabulation fills it in a fixed order from the smallest index upward — both visit each subproblem once
items.explanation_md: They're two directions through the same table; memoization can skip subproblems the input never needs, which is its main practical edge.

items.prompt_md: Why can the O(n) array-based climbing-stairs solution be shrunk to use only two variables instead of a full dp array?
options: Because dp[i] only ever reads dp[i-1] and dp[i-2], so once those two are known, everything before them can be discarded
Because Python arrays are always slower than plain variables
Because the final answer only depends on dp[n], not on any intermediate value
Because two variables can store more information than an array of the same size
items.answer: Because dp[i] only ever reads dp[i-1] and dp[i-2], so once those two are known, everything before them can be discarded
items.explanation_md: A fixed, small lookback window is precisely what lets tabulation roll forward with O(1) state instead of O(n).

items.prompt_md: A buggy climb_stairs implementation sets dp[1] = 1, dp[2] = 3 instead of 1 and 2, then applies the correct recurrence for i >= 3. What kind of bug is this?
options: A wrong base case, which will silently corrupt every later dp value even though the recurrence itself is correct
An off-by-one error in the loop's range()
A missing memo cache causing exponential blowup
An integer overflow in the addition
items.answer: A wrong base case, which will silently corrupt every later dp value even though the recurrence itself is correct
items.explanation_md: Because every later value is built from the base cases, a wrong seed propagates forward and corrupts the whole table without raising an error.

items.prompt_md: In a Fibonacci-style dp loop written as `for i in range(3, n + 1): dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]`, what bug results from writing `range(3, n)` instead?
options: dp[n] never gets computed, so the function returns a stale or default value instead of the real answer
The loop raises an IndexError immediately
dp[1] and dp[2] get overwritten incorrectly
The recurrence starts adding three previous terms instead of two
items.answer: dp[n] never gets computed, so the function returns a stale or default value instead of the real answer
items.explanation_md: range(3, n) stops one short of n, an off-by-one that leaves the very last cell — the one being returned — unfilled.

items.prompt_md: Why is 'overlapping subproblems plus optimal substructure' the right justification for using DP on climbing stairs, rather than just 'it involves counting'?
options: Counting alone doesn't guarantee reuse; it's specifically that ways(i-1) and ways(i-2) get needed again and again across the recursion tree that makes caching pay off
DP is required any time a problem involves an integer answer
Overlapping subproblems only matters for graph algorithms, not counting problems
Optimal substructure means the answer must be a single number rather than a list
items.answer: Counting alone doesn't guarantee reuse; it's specifically that ways(i-1) and ways(i-2) get needed again and again across the recursion tree that makes caching pay off
items.explanation_md: DP earns its keep specifically when subproblems recur; a counting problem whose subproblems never repeat gains nothing from caching.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the naive, non-memoized recursive climbing-stairs solution?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
items.explanation_md: Each call branches into two more calls with no caching, producing a full binary call tree of depth n.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the bottom-up tabulated climbing-stairs solution using a full dp array?
options: O(n) time, O(n) space
O(n) time, O(1) space
O(n^2) time, O(n) space
O(log n) time, O(1) space
items.answer: O(n) time, O(n) space
items.explanation_md: One pass fills n cells, and the array itself holds n entries; rolling variables are an optional further optimization.

items.prompt_md: After collapsing the dp array down to two rolling variables (prev1, prev2), what is the space complexity of the climbing-stairs solution?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
items.explanation_md: Only two scalar variables are kept alive at any point, regardless of how large n grows.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of memoized top-down recursion for this recurrence (e.g. via @lru_cache), counting each distinct argument value only once?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: There are only n distinct subproblems, and the cache guarantees each is computed exactly once, with O(1) work per subproblem.

items.prompt_md: A problem says: count the number of distinct ways to climb a staircase of n steps, taking either 1 or 2 steps at a time. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP: define dp[i] as the ways to reach step i, built from dp[i-1] and dp[i-2]
Greedy: always take the largest step size available
Binary search over the number of steps taken
Two pointers scanning from both ends of the staircase
items.answer: 1-D DP: define dp[i] as the ways to reach step i, built from dp[i-1] and dp[i-2]
items.explanation_md: Counting the ways to reach a target with a small fixed lookback is the classic overlapping-subproblems signature that DP is built for.

items.prompt_md: You need the number of distinct ways to tile a 2-by-n board using 1x2 dominoes. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP: define dp[i] as the tilings of a 2-by-i board, related to dp[i-1] and dp[i-2]
Backtracking: place dominoes one at a time and undo on failure, exploring the full search tree
Binary search over the number of dominoes used
Sort the board positions and greedily fill from the left
items.answer: 1-D DP: define dp[i] as the tilings of a 2-by-i board, related to dp[i-1] and dp[i-2]
items.explanation_md: This is Fibonacci-shaped: the last column is either a vertical domino (reducing to width i-1) or two horizontal ones (reducing to width i-2).

items.prompt_md: You must find the minimum cost to climb a staircase where each step has an associated cost, and you can start from step 0 or 1 and take 1 or 2 steps at a time. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP: define dp[i] as the min cost to reach step i, taking the cheaper of dp[i-1] and dp[i-2] plus that step's cost
Greedy: always take whichever single next step currently has the lower cost
Backtracking over every subset of steps taken
Two pointers moving inward from both ends of the staircase
items.answer: 1-D DP: define dp[i] as the min cost to reach step i, taking the cheaper of dp[i-1] and dp[i-2] plus that step's cost
items.explanation_md: A greedy 'cheapest immediate step' choice can lock you into an expensive step later; DP weighs both full paths to i-1 and i-2.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array of distinct integers, find the index of a specific target value as fast as possible. Which approach fits?
options: Binary search: repeatedly halve the search range using the sorted order
1-D DP: define dp[i] as whether the target is found by index i
Backtracking: try every index and undo on mismatch
Greedy: scan left to right and stop at the first larger element
items.answer: Binary search: repeatedly halve the search range using the sorted order
items.explanation_md: This is a trap for DP pattern-matching: there's no overlapping-subproblem or optimal-substructure question here, just a sorted-order search — binary search is strictly the right tool.

items.prompt_md: You need to count the number of ways to reach the top of a staircase where a single move can be any of several fixed step sizes (e.g. 1, 3, or 5). Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP: define dp[i] as ways to reach step i, summing dp[i-s] over every allowed step size s
Two pointers, one starting from each end of the staircase
Binary search over the possible step sizes
Greedy: always take the largest allowed step size first
items.answer: 1-D DP: define dp[i] as ways to reach step i, summing dp[i-s] over every allowed step size s
items.explanation_md: More step sizes just widens the lookback window in the same recurrence shape — still overlapping subproblems, still solved by a 1-D table.

items.prompt_md: You're given a sorted array and must find two numbers in it that add up exactly to a target value. Which approach fits?
options: Two pointers: one at each end of the sorted array, moving inward based on whether the current sum is too small or too large
1-D DP: define dp[i] as whether a pair summing to the target exists using the first i elements
Backtracking over every possible pair
Binary search independently for each possible first element
items.answer: Two pointers: one at each end of the sorted array, moving inward based on whether the current sum is too small or too large
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: the array being sorted with a single target sum is the two-pointers fingerprint, not a DP one — building a dp table here would be unnecessary overhead for a problem two pointers solves in one linear pass.

items.prompt_md: You must generate every possible sequence of step sizes that climbs a staircase of n steps, not just count how many exist. Which approach fits?
options: Backtracking: recursively try each step size, append it to the current path, and undo before trying the next, collecting complete paths
1-D DP: define dp[i] as the count of ways to reach step i
Binary search over the sequence length
Two pointers converging on the middle step
items.answer: Backtracking: recursively try each step size, append it to the current path, and undo before trying the next, collecting complete paths
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: DP collapses ways into a single count and can't reconstruct every distinct sequence, so enumerating actual paths calls for backtracking instead.

items.prompt_md: You must count the number of ways to decode a string of digits into letters, where each digit maps to one letter and certain adjacent digit pairs also map to a single letter. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP: define dp[i] as the ways to decode the first i characters, adding dp[i-1] and/or dp[i-2] depending on which decodings are valid at position i
Greedy: always decode the longest valid prefix first
Two pointers scanning from both ends of the string
Binary search over the possible decoding lengths
items.answer: 1-D DP: define dp[i] as the ways to decode the first i characters, adding dp[i-1] and/or dp[i-2] depending on which decodings are valid at position i
items.explanation_md: Whether position i extends a one-digit or two-digit decoding is exactly a lookback of one or two, the same overlapping-subproblem shape as climbing stairs.

items.prompt_md: You're given two strings and must determine whether one is an anagram of the other (same letters, same counts, any order). Which approach fits?
options: Count letter frequencies in both strings with a hash map and compare the counts
1-D DP: define dp[i] as whether the first i characters can be rearranged into an anagram
Backtracking: try every permutation of one string and compare to the other
Two pointers scanning both strings from the same starting index
items.answer: Count letter frequencies in both strings with a hash map and compare the counts
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: there's no optimal-substructure decision to make here, just a frequency comparison — a hash map (or fixed-size count array) solves it directly with no need for a dp table.

items.prompt_md: A fence has n posts, and you must count the number of ways to paint each post using either of two colors such that no more than two adjacent posts share the same color. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP: define dp[i] as the valid colorings of the first i posts, built from dp[i-1] and dp[i-2] weighted by same-color and different-color transitions
Binary search over the number of color changes
Two pointers tracking the start and end of same-color runs
Backtracking over every possible coloring of all n posts
items.answer: 1-D DP: define dp[i] as the valid colorings of the first i posts, built from dp[i-1] and dp[i-2] weighted by same-color and different-color transitions
items.explanation_md: The count at post i depends only on the coloring counts ending at i-1 and i-2 (same-color-run length matters, not full history), which is the fixed-lookback DP shape.
